CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is adequately organized (Related Work → Method → Experiments) and states its main idea (jointly learning sememe, sense, and word embeddings) early on. Notation is introduced (X, S, W, s(w)i, x(si)j, C(w)) and used consistently in the methodology, but the presentation suffers from extraction/formatting noise and occasional repetition (many duplicated sentences/citations and LaTeX artifacts) that make some paragraphs hard to follow. Figures and examples (e.g. Fig.1/3, Havana case study) are informative in intent, but the actual figure references and table descriptions are sometimes disconnected from text. Writing is generally understandable but has grammatical/formatting issues and redundant text that would benefit from careful copy-editing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — this is squarely within top NLP/ML venue scope: it proposes sememe-augmented word/sense/sememe embeddings using HowNet and attention (SSA, SAC, SAT) and builds on standard WRL literature (Skip-gram/CBOW/GloVe, multi-prototype/sense work). The intended audience (representation learning and lexical semantics) is appropriate and the problem formulation (learning sememe-regularized embeddings) matches venue expectations. Methods and evaluations use widely accepted baselines and metrics (word similarity, word analogy, case WSD studies), though evaluations appear mainly intrinsic and focused on HowNet/Chinese, which is a minor scope limitation for a top-tier conference.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—incorporating fine-grained semantic units (sememes) into word representation learning—to better model polysemy and provide interpretable embeddings (it jointly learns sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet; Sec.3). The attention-based SE-WRL variants (SAC, SAT) explicitly perform context-sensitive sense/sememe selection and are shown to improve word similarity and analogy benchmarks and enable WSD case studies (Sec.3.3, Sec.4), so the results are likely to change how researchers integrate lexical knowledge into embeddings. While dependence on HowNet (a language-specific, manually annotated resource) limits immediate universal adoption, the framework opens meaningful new directions for leveraging structured semantic atoms and is likely to be built upon in subsequent work across representation learning and downstream NLP tasks.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a notable and distinguishable idea: incorporating HowNet sememes to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings and using attention (SSA, SAC, SAT) to perform context-aware sense/sememe selection (sections 3.1–3.3). This is differentiated from prior multi-prototype and synset-based work (Huang et al. 2012; Chen et al. 2014; Rothe & Schütze 2015) because it leverages sememe annotations as explicit semantic units and integrates them into Skip-gram with attention for WSD and WRL. The technique is a non-trivial extension (semantic regularization + attention over sememes) rather than a simple reapplication of existing methods, so it is more than incremental but not a radical new paradigm.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states its main claims (sememe-augmented embeddings with SSA/SAC/SAT improve WRL and enable context-sensitive sense selection) and provides clear model formulas (Eqs. 3, 7–9) and reasonable training protocols and baselines (Skip‑gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) and evaluation metrics (Spearman for similarity, accuracy/mean rank for analogy). However, the provided text does not include numeric experimental results, statistical significance tests, or detailed dataset descriptions to substantiate the claims, and some simplifying assumptions (ignoring sememe relations, fixed K′=2) are not justified experimentally. Mathematically the formulations are consistent and implementation choices are standard, but the empirical support is incompletely reported in the excerpt, so conclusions are only partially verifiable.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates its sememe-augmented models on standard intrinsic tasks (word similarity with Spearman correlation and a Chinese word analogy benchmark) and compares against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), and includes qualitative case studies showing attention-based WSD, with many training hyperparameters reported (embedding size, window, negatives, K′). However, experiments are limited to intrinsic evaluations and a few qualitative WSD examples (no quantitative WSD or downstream/extrinsic tasks), there are no systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of K′, negative samples, or removing sememe components), and failure modes/limitations are scarcely discussed beyond a simplification of HowNet structure. Reproducibility is partially supported by equations and hyperparameters but lacks full dataset/corpus and training-run details (epochs, corpus size, seeds).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe) and includes a task-specific baseline MST inspired by Chen et al. (Section 4.2: “As for baselines...Skip-gram, CBOW and GloVe” and “we implement another baseline Maximum Selection...inspired by (Chen et al., 2014)”), and it states that models were trained under the same experimental settings for fairness. It also cites and positions itself relative to many relevant prior approaches (multi-prototype methods, Neelakantan et al., Rothe & Schütze, WordNet/Chen et al.), but it omits direct empirical comparisons to some sense-aware recent methods and provides limited discussion when prior methods might perform comparably or better and limited post-hoc analysis of differences in results. Overall the comparisons are good and mostly fair, with minor omissions and limited critical discussion.
